questions.title: How should `verify` and `verify:full` relate?
questions.context: Root `package.json:11` `verify` skips many package tests; CI runs `verify:full` in `.github/workflows/verify.yml:31`. Local-vs-CI divergence is what hid the no-net failure.

options.label: Subset relationship + contract test
options.description: Keep two scripts but add a contract test that asserts every package test that runs in `verify:full` also runs (or is explicitly excluded with rationale) in `verify`. Drift becomes a test failure.

options.label: Collapse to one script
options.description: Drop tiered verify entirely. Local devs run the same thing CI runs. Slower local feedback but zero divergence risk.

options.label: CI runs both, asserts no new skips
options.description: Keep two scripts, but CI runs `verify` first then `verify:full`, and a contract test asserts no package was added to a skip-list since the last release.

questions.title: How to fix the no-net violation in `@protostar/mechanical-checks`?
questions.context: `packages/mechanical-checks/src/no-net.contract.test.ts:43` forbids `isomorphic-git`, but production imports it in `packages/mechanical-checks/src/diff-name-only.ts:1`. Tier in `package.json:36` and `AGENTS.md:26` says pure.

options.label: Move git inspection into `@protostar/repo`
options.description: `diff-name-only` becomes a repo-runtime helper. Mechanical-checks stays a pure transform, takes pre-computed diff names as input. Strongest separation; small wiring change in factory-cli.

options.label: Inject `diffNameOnly` evidence into mechanical-checks
options.description: Repo runner produces diff-name evidence and passes it in via the existing injected-evidence pattern. Mechanical-checks remains pure (just consumes a value), no import flip needed.

options.label: Flip mechanical-checks tier to subprocess
options.description: Acknowledge the implementation reality. Update `package.json:36`, `AGENTS.md:26`, and `authority-boundary.contract.test.ts` to declare mechanical-checks as `subprocess`. Loosens authority guarantee for this package.

questions.title: Where should configured mechanical commands run?
questions.context: Currently: config in `factory-config.schema.json:187`, list in `wiring/review-loop.ts:214`, raw `spawn` in `main.ts:1892`. Bypasses `@protostar/repo`'s allowlist + per-command schema + refusal evidence.

options.label: Through `@protostar/repo` runner — closed allowlist
options.description: Mechanical commands resolve to a fixed set of repo-allowlisted CLIs (`pnpm`, `tsc`, `node`, `git`). Operator picks from the menu by name; argv shape is schema-validated per-command. Strongest authority; least flexible.

options.label: Through repo runner — operator-declared, schema-validated
options.description: Operator declares `{ name, argv[] }` in factory-config; schema requires shape (no shell strings, no env vars in argv). Runner intersects with repo allowlist at admission; refuses if not allowed. Flexible but operator can declare new commands.

options.label: Through repo runner — capability-bound
options.description: Each declared mechanical command requires explicit capability in `confirmedIntent.capabilityEnvelope.subprocess.allow[]`. No command runs without being signed into the intent. Most authority-tight; bumps confirmed-intent schema.

questions.title: Does this require a confirmedIntent schema bump (1.5.0 → 1.6.0)?
questions.context: Current schema is 1.5.0 (Phase 7). If mechanical commands become capability-bound (Q-03 option c) the envelope grows a `mechanical: { allowed: [...] }` field.

options.label: Yes — bump to 1.6.0 with `mechanical.allowed`
options.description: Mechanical commands become first-class capability. Fixture cascade required (every signed-intent fixture re-signs). Operator must list mechanical commands at intent time.

options.label: No — reuse `subprocess.allow`
options.description: Mechanical commands intersect with the existing subprocess allow list. No schema bump; same authority surface as other subprocess calls. Less explicit but simpler.

options.label: Defer — depends on Q-03 outcome
options.description: Bump only if Q-03 picks the capability-bound path. Otherwise no bump.

questions.title: Where does the mechanical command run?
questions.context: Today raw `spawn` runs in target-repo cwd. With env scrubbing (Q-06) we also have to decide whether the command sees the cloned workspace or a sandboxed cwd.

options.label: Cloned target-repo workspace
options.description: Same as today — the command runs against the cloned repo. Required for `pnpm verify` to inspect the target.

options.label: Read-only bind-mount of workspace
options.description: Mechanical commands cannot write back. Defends against a target-repo `verify` script writing arbitrary files.

options.label: Workspace cwd + post-run dirty-tree assertion
options.description: Run in workspace; after run, repo runner asserts no unexpected file writes (existing `dirtyWorktreeStatus` check, scoped to mechanical phase).

questions.title: What is the minimum env baseline for `packages/repo/src/subprocess-runner.ts`?
questions.context: Both call sites (`:33` and `:85`) default child env to `process.env`. Delivery uses `PROTOSTAR_GITHUB_TOKEN` from process env in `main.ts:1198`. Empty `{}` would break commands needing PATH.

options.label: Empty `{}` — caller passes everything
options.description: Most defensive. Caller must explicitly include PATH, HOME, NODE_OPTIONS, etc. High friction but zero ambient leakage.

options.label: Tiny baseline (PATH, HOME, LANG, USER)
options.description: Posix-required env only. Caller adds secrets explicitly. Practical default; covers 90% of subprocess needs without leaking project-specific env.

options.label: Baseline + `inheritEnv: string[]` allowlist
options.description: Same as (b) plus an explicit per-call allowlist of vars to inherit by name. Fine-grained; logs the allowlist into evidence.

questions.title: How does delivery opt in to `PROTOSTAR_GITHUB_TOKEN`?
questions.context: Delivery is the only place a real secret needs to flow into a subprocess. Today it inherits ambient env. We want explicit opt-in.

options.label: Token passed only at Octokit boundary, never to subprocess
options.description: Delivery uses Octokit (HTTP) for PR ops and `pushBranch` via `isomorphic-git` `onAuth` shim — both library calls, not subprocess. Confirms subprocess never needs the token. Strongest separation.

options.label: `secretEnv: { TOKEN }` map with redacted logging
options.description: Subprocess runner accepts a separate `secretEnv` field; values are passed to child but logged as `<redacted:TOKEN>` in evidence. Generic mechanism for any future secret.

options.label: Token-bearing AuthorizedOp brand
options.description: A new branded type the runner consumes. Authority-typed; type system prevents leak. Highest ceremony.

questions.title: Should persisted logs be scrubbed for secret patterns as defense-in-depth?
questions.context: Even with empty env, a target repo's script could echo a token if the operator runs the factory with the token in their shell.

options.label: Yes — redaction filter on log write
options.description: Apply known patterns (GH PAT shapes, bearer headers, JWT shapes) before writing rolling tail / evidence. Defense in depth.

options.label: No — env scrubbing is enough
options.description: Redaction is theatre once env is empty. Adds maintenance burden (pattern drift) without solving a real attack.

options.label: Both — empty env + redaction filter
options.description: Belt and suspenders. Filter cost is small; pattern set is small (we control what tokens look like in our factory).

questions.title: Where is the path/op/diff agreement enforced?
questions.context: `applyChangeSet` counts `PatchRequest.path` (`:65`) but reads/writes `patch.op` (`:82`, `:114`). Display vs write target can disagree at the API boundary.

options.label: Inside `applyChangeSet` — refuse at function entry
options.description: Function asserts `path === op.path === parsedDiff.fileName` and refuses with a typed error if not. Library-level safety; every caller protected.

options.label: At admission — `PatchRequest` brand only constructible if invariant holds
options.description: Move the invariant up. Branded constructor refuses; once you have a `PatchRequest` you know the paths agree. `applyChangeSet` trusts the brand.

options.label: Both — admission constructs + applyChangeSet re-asserts
options.description: Admission is the primary gate; `applyChangeSet` re-asserts as defense-in-depth (catches handcrafted `PatchRequest` instances in tests).

questions.title: How permissive is 'agree'?
questions.context: `./foo/bar.ts` vs `foo/bar.ts` vs `/abs/foo/bar.ts` — string equality fails on equivalent paths.

options.label: Exact string equality after canonicalization
options.description: All three sources normalize via the same canonicalize-relative-path helper, then require strict equality. Predictable; rejects ambiguous patches.

options.label: Path-equivalent (resolve+normalize)
options.description: Compare via `path.resolve` against workspace root. Accepts cosmetic differences; needs a workspace-root anchor.

options.label: Display path advisory, only `op.path` and parsed-diff filename must match
options.description: Loosen the cosmetic gate. Display is for humans; enforcement is on the two real write inputs.

questions.title: Which artifact is the source of truth for tier?
questions.context: Three artifacts encode tiers today: `protostar.tier` in `package.json`, the AGENTS.md table, and `authority-boundary.contract.test.ts`. Two of them disagree about `evaluation-runner` and `mechanical-checks`.

options.label: `package.json` `protostar.tier`
options.description: Manifest is canonical (lives next to code; package owns its claim). AGENTS.md and contract test derive from it.

options.label: Authority-boundary contract test
options.description: Code-as-spec — the contract is the runtime gate. Manifest must match the contract.

options.label: AGENTS.md table
options.description: Human-readable canonical. Agents and humans read the same source. Both manifest and contract test asserted against parsed AGENTS.md.

questions.title: How is drift detected?
questions.context: Phase 10.1.7 already has `tier-conformance.contract.test.ts` (manifest-driven). It does not currently parse AGENTS.md.

options.label: Extend `tier-conformance.contract.test.ts`
options.description: Add AGENTS.md parsing; assert manifest tier == AGENTS.md table tier == authority-boundary contract entry. One test, three sources.

options.label: `tools/check-tier-coherence.ts` wired into `verify`
options.description: Standalone script. Easier to run in pre-commit; less reportable than a node:test failure.

options.label: Generate AGENTS.md table from manifests
options.description: Single source — manifest. `tools/regen-agents-tiers.ts` rewrites the table; CI asserts table matches generated form. Eliminates drift by construction.

options.label: Network — match manifest
options.description: Update authority-boundary contract to reflect manifest. Evaluation runs LM Studio HTTP calls — `network` is honest.

options.label: Pure — match contract
options.description: Update manifest to `pure`. Evaluation-runner is a pure orchestrator; network is done by the injected adapter, not evaluation-runner itself.

options.label: Re-derive from imports
options.description: Inspect actual imports; resolve to whichever the code currently does. Don't pre-judge — code is truth.

questions.title: Decomposition of large files — defer entirely?
questions.context: `packages/planning/src/index.ts:1` is 5701 LOC; `apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:1` is 3429 LOC. Architecture read flagged them but recommended deferring until after authority is stable.

options.label: Defer entirely — Phase 12 does zero file splits
options.description: Strict scope. Authority fixes only. Splits become Phase 13.

options.label: Pull command-execution wiring out of main.ts only-if-needed
options.description: Allowed exception: if routing mechanical commands through repo runner is cleaner with a separate `wiring/mechanical-runner.ts`, do that. No other splits.

options.label: Light decomposition allowed
options.description: Pull all three wiring concerns (command-execution, review-loop, delivery) out of main.ts as part of the authority fix. Bigger blast radius; pays off the architectural debt now.

questions.title: What proves Phase 12 is done?
questions.context: Phase 10 dogfood loop is the existing live verification surface. Phase 11 has stress harness.

options.label: Contract tests + `verify:full` green
options.description: All new contracts (verify-parity, mechanical-via-repo, env-empty-default, applyChangeSet-invariant, tier-coherence) pass. No live re-run required.

options.label: Above + Phase 10 dogfood re-run on protostar-toy-ttt
options.description: Re-run the existing dogfood loop end-to-end. Confirms env scrubbing didn't break delivery, mechanical routing didn't break review.

options.label: Above + secret-leak attack test
options.description: Add an offensive test: target repo's `pnpm verify` echoes `$PROTOSTAR_GITHUB_TOKEN`; assert evidence logs do not contain the token shape.

questions.title: Does Phase 12 block Phase 11?
questions.context: Phase 11 is `Headless Mode + E2E Stress`. Phase 12 was just inserted with `Depends on: 10, 11`. If Phase 11 hasn't started yet, ordering is open.

options.label: Phase 12 runs after Phase 11 (as-roadmapped)
options.description: Phase 11 first — stress harness exposes more authority-boundary issues to fold in. Phase 12 then sweeps everything.

options.label: Phase 12 runs BEFORE Phase 11 — invert dependency
options.description: Authority is broken NOW (CI red, mechanical bypass, env leak). Fix it before stress-testing the broken surface. Update ROADMAP.md `Depends on` to `10` only.

options.label: Run in parallel — independent workstreams
options.description: Phase 12 in a workstream branch, Phase 11 in another. Merge ordering by whoever finishes first; rebase the second one.
